text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
MAINTENANCE THERAPY COMPLETION / SURVEILLANCE TRANSITION

ATTENDING: [Gynecologic Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Return outpatient visit

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Margaret Osei is a 55-year-old postmenopausal female with FIGO IIIC (pT3c pN0 pM0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary, bilateral, with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), presenting today for her planned 36-month post-chemotherapy visit. This visit marks the scheduled completion of nira (Zejula) maintenance therapy per the SOLO1 protocol framework. She continues in complete clinical remission.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei was originally diagnosed approximately three years ago with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with peritoneal carcinomatosis and omental caking, presenting with significant ascites (CA-125 1,847 U/mL at diagnosis). She underwent optimal primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0) — procedures included TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, and right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy — followed by six cycles of adjuvant carbo (AUC 5) / PTX (Taxol, 175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 in cycles 4-6 due to grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy). She achieved a complete clinical and radiographic response after completing chemotherapy, with CA-125 normalizing to 11 U/mL and CT imaging demonstrating no residual disease. Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and HRD-positive tumor, she was initiated on Zejula 200 mg daily as maintenance therapy, with subsequent dose reduction to 100 mg daily after developing grade 3 thrombocytopenia at week 6 (nadir platelet count 41 x10^9/L). She has now been on nira 100 mg daily continuously for approximately 30 months without further grade 3+ hematologic events. Today represents the planned endpoint of maintenance therapy at 36 months total duration.

She reports feeling well overall. She has returned to full-time work as a high school biology teacher and describes her quality of life as excellent. She denies any new or concerning symptoms. She reports no abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, change in bowel habits, or pelvic discomfort. No dyspnea, cough, or chest pain. No new neurologic symptoms. She notes mild residual cognitive fogginess, which she attributes to chemotherapy-related cognitive effects, though she says this has steadily improved and is no longer meaningfully disruptive. Hot flashes are well-controlled on venlafaxine. She uses topical vaginal estrogen for vaginal dryness with good effect. She took her last Zejula dose this morning.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Energy level good.
- HEENT: No headaches, vision changes, or oral lesions.
- Respiratory: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Blood pressure has been well-controlled on home monitoring.
- Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain, nausea (previously grade 1 on nira, now resolved), bloating, change in bowel habits, or rectal bleeding.
- Genitourinary: No urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, or hematuria. No pelvic pressure or vaginal discharge.
- Musculoskeletal: No joint pain or myalgias. No bone pain.
- Neurologic: Peripheral neuropathy fully resolved (previously grade 2 during PTX). No new neurologic complaints. Mild cognitive changes as noted above, improving.
- Hematologic: No easy bruising, petechiae, or bleeding since Zejula dose was reduced. No epistaxis or gingival bleeding.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Menopause symptoms managed as above. No new endocrine concerns.
- Psychiatric: Mood good. No depression or anxiety. Patient reports feeling empowered and engaged with her health.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 68 kg, BMI 24.1 kg/m2. ECOG performance status: 0.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears her stated age.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable masses or organomegaly. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed, non-tender. No ascites appreciated on exam (no shifting dullness, no fluid wave).
Pelvic: Vaginal cuff intact, no masses, no tenderness. No palpable pelvic or adnexal masses. Normal post-hysterectomy exam.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Sensory exam intact to light touch in bilateral upper and lower extremities — no residual neuropathy detected. Coordination intact, gait normal.
Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or ecchymoses. No suspicious skin lesions.
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
- CA-125: 11 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL; stable; previously 10 U/mL at 24-month visit)
- CBC: WBC 5.1 x10^9/L (normal); Hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1, improving — previously 11.9 g/dL); Platelets 141 x10^9/L (Grade 1, stable throughout maintenance)
- CMP: Sodium 139, Potassium 4.1, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), BUN 14, ALT 18 U/L, AST 22 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL — all within normal limits
- Lipids: Total cholesterol 198 mg/dL, LDL 118 mg/dL, HDL 54 mg/dL, Triglycerides 142 mg/dL — acceptable

---

IMAGING:
Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained approximately 3 months prior, at 24-month surveillance interval): No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, no ascites, no pelvic or abdominal masses, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions. Stable post-surgical changes. Continued complete radiographic remission.

Next surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis is planned in approximately 3 months (scheduled as part of transition to post-maintenance surveillance schedule).

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. FIGO IIIC (pT3c pN0 pM0) high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, bilateral, with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC) and HRD-positive tumor — COMPLETE SUSTAINED REMISSION, now 36 months post-completion of adjuvant carbo/PTX.

2. Zejula (Zejula) maintenance therapy — COMPLETED TODAY as planned, having achieved the pre-specified 36-month maintenance duration consistent with SOLO1 trial protocol guidance for first-line BRCA-mutated ovarian cancer. Maintenance therapy was tolerated acceptably with dose reduction required to 100 mg daily due to grade 3 thrombocytopenia early in the course; she has been on 100 mg daily for approximately 30 months without further significant hematologic toxicity.

3. Grade 3 thrombocytopenia, resolved — distant history, occurred early on nira at 200 mg dose. Platelet counts have been stable in the Grade 1 range on 100 mg dosing throughout.

4. Peripheral sensory neuropathy — fully resolved. Previously grade 2 during carbo/PTX (cycles 3-6). No residual findings on today's examination.

5. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant — genetic counseling completed; family cascade testing underway. Daughter (age 27) confirmed to carry variant and has been referred to appropriate high-risk surveillance and risk-reduction consultation.

6. Menopause symptom management — adequate control with venlafaxine for vasomotor symptoms and topical vaginal estrogen for genitourinary syndrome of menopause. No systemic HRT given oncologic history. Will continue current regimen.

---

PLAN:

1. nira DISCONTINUATION: Today marks the planned completion of nira maintenance. No further doses will be taken. Patient counseled that discontinuation at 36 months is the planned endpoint based on current trial data and is not an indication of treatment failure. She is strongly encouraged to report any new symptoms promptly, particularly abdominal bloating, increasing girth, early satiety, pelvic pressure, or new pain — all of which warrant urgent evaluation rather than watchful waiting.

2. SURVEILLANCE SCHEDULE — TRANSITIONING TO POST-MAINTENANCE MONITORING:
   - CA-125 every 3 months for years 4-5 post-chemotherapy, then every 6 months thereafter
   - CT abdomen/pelvis: scheduled in 3 months (first post-maintenance scan), then annually or sooner if clinically indicated
   - CT chest: as clinically indicated or if CA-125 elevation detected
   - Clinical examination every 6 months
   - No role for routine PET-CT in surveillance at this time

3. COUNSELING REGARDING RECURRENCE RISK: Patient was counseled that while she has achieved and maintained a complete remission over three years — a favorable prognostic sign, particularly in the context of germline BRCA1 mutation and initial complete response — the lifetime risk of relapse in stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian cancer remains real. Recurrence can occur even after prolonged remission, and she should be aware of the symptoms of recurrence. Discussed that at time of any confirmed recurrence, given her platinum-free interval and BRCA1 status, additional effective therapy options would be available.

4. ONGOING MEDICATIONS:
   - Venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily (vasomotor symptoms) — continue
   - Topical estradiol vaginal cream (low-dose, local application) — continue
   - Ondansetron PRN — discontinue (no longer needed off Zejula)
   - Calcium/vitamin D supplementation — continue
   - Gabapentin (initiated previously for neuropathy) — has been self-tapered and discontinued per patient report; neuropathy resolved

5. RETURN TO CARE: Follow-up in 3 months with CA-125 and CT abdomen/pelvis. Patient instructed to call sooner for any new or concerning symptoms. She is fully aware of warning signs and expressed confidence in understanding her follow-up plan.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL: Patient is in excellent spirits. She has resumed her full professional role and is engaged with a local ovarian cancer advocacy group. She expressed gratitude for her care and interest in participating in future clinical research if she remains in remission. Referred to our survivorship clinic for comprehensive survivorship care planning, including bone density assessment (given surgical menopause), cardiovascular risk assessment, and ongoing psychosocial support.

Time spent: 45 minutes total encounter, >50% in counseling and coordination of care.

[Signed: Attending Gynecologic Oncologist]
